content:
I'm architecting NVIDIA BlueField (DPF) support for the OPI DPU Operator, and I'll drive this, you're my implementation partner and critical reviewer, not the architect. I've already read both codebases end to end, so hold everything to REAL types; if I ever hand you something that doesn't map to actual source, push back.

Context I've established from the source:
- The OPI DPU Operator (openshift/dpu-operator, mirrored at opiproject/dpu-operator) does NOT touch hardware directly. It integrates each vendor through a per-node "Vendor Specific Plugin" (VSP) spoken over gRPC on a unix socket. Intel and Marvell each ship a VSP today. That VSP seam is the correct place to add NVIDIA.
- NVIDIA already ships DPF (nvidia/doca-platform), a full cluster-scoped operator that provisions BlueField-3 and runs accelerated OVN-Kubernetes offloaded to the DPU. The whole point of this exercise is to REUSE DPF wholesale, not reimplement offload.

I'll paste the exact interfaces and CRDs next so we work from ground truth. Confirm you understand the framing and are ready for the grounding.

Understood, and agreed on the framing: NVIDIA enters at the existing per-node VSP seam, and DPF is reused as the offload/provisioning engine rather than reimplemented. I'll hold every proposal to the real types you paste and flag anything that doesn't map to actual source. Send the VSP contract and the DPF CRD groups.

Ground truth I pulled from the repos today.

OPI DPU Operator, internal/daemon/plugin.VendorPlugin (Go):

    type VendorPlugin interface {
        Start(ctx context.Context) (string, int32, error)   // -> LifeCycleService.Init
        Close()
        CreateBridgePort(*opi.CreateBridgePortRequest) (*opi.BridgePort, error)
        DeleteBridgePort(*opi.DeleteBridgePortRequest) error
        CreateNetworkFunction(input, output, bridgeID string) error
        DeleteNetworkFunction(input, output, bridgeID string) error
        GetDevices() (*pb.DeviceListResponse, error)
        SetNumVfs(vfCount int32) (*pb.VfCount, error)
        SetDpuNetworkConfig(isAccelerated bool) error
    }

Wire contract dpu-api/api.proto (package Vendor): LifeCycleService.Init(dpu_mode, dpu_identifier)->IpPort; DeviceService{GetDevices, SetNumVfs}; NetworkFunctionService{Create/DeleteNetworkFunction}; DpuNetworkConfigService.SetDpuNetworkConfig; HeartbeatService.Ping; plus the OPI EVPN-GW BridgePortService from opiproject/opi-api. Important detail I want you to register: the VSP contract is itself partly built on OPI's own vendor-neutral API (BridgePort), so the seam is already vendor-agnostic by design.

DPU Operator intent CRDs (api/v1): DpuOperatorConfig (cluster-scoped, minimal spec), DataProcessingUnit, DpuNetwork, ServiceFunctionChain (Spec = NodeSelector + []NetworkFunction{Name, Image}).

DPF CRDs (nvidia/doca-platform):
- operator.dpu.nvidia.com: DPFOperatorConfig (readiness via status.conditions, TypeReady / SystemComponentsReady; there is NO provisioningReady field, note that)
- provisioning.dpu.nvidia.com: BFB, DPUFlavor, DPUSet, DPU, DPUCluster
- svc.dpu.nvidia.com: DPUService, DPUServiceChain, DPUServiceInterface, DPUServiceIPAM, DPUServiceNAD, DPUDeployment

Read those back to me in one line each so I know we're aligned, and confirm you see the two structurally different shapes here.

Aligned, reading back:
- VSP seam = per-node, imperative gRPC (Init/GetDevices/SetNumVfs/Create-DeleteNetworkFunction/SetDpuNetworkConfig) + OPI EVPN-GW BridgePort, vendor-neutral by construction.
- DPU-Op intent CRDs = DpuOperatorConfig / DpuNetwork / ServiceFunctionChain (flat {name,image} NF list + NodeSelector).
- DPF = DPFOperatorConfig (readiness via status.conditions, no provisioningReady), provisioning group (BFB/DPUFlavor/DPUSet/DPU/DPUCluster), and svc group (DPUService/DPUServiceChain/DPUServiceInterface/…).

Two shapes are clear and, importantly, different: the VSP is per-node and imperative; DPF is cluster-scoped and declarative. I'll keep readiness references to status.conditions, not an invented field.

Correct. Here's my core architectural finding, and I want you to actively try to break it, not agree reflexively:

A pure VSP adapter is necessary but NOT sufficient to maximize DPF reuse. The decisive axis is SCOPE, not latency, and don't reach for the sync-vs-async argument, because I already know Start()/Init has a retry loop, so "slow init" isn't the real problem. The real mismatch: a per-node imperative plugin is the wrong SHAPE to own DPF's cluster-scoped, minutes-long, declarative provisioning lifecycle (BFB flash + DPUCluster formation). Force all of DPF through the per-node VSP straw and you bottleneck the exact capability you came to reuse.

So I'm splitting the integration by concern, each concern mapped to exactly ONE of the three named patterns:
1. Sub-operator, owns DPF install + provisioning lifecycle (DPFOperatorConfig + DPUSet/BFB/DPUFlavor). Cluster-scoped, async.
2. Adapter (thin NVIDIA VSP), only the node-local surface (GetDevices/SetNumVfs/NF wiring). Init() does no provisioning; it gates on DPF readiness.
3. CRD-translation reconciler, maps DPU-Operator intent (ServiceFunctionChain, DpuNetwork) to DPF CRDs (DPUServiceChain, DPUServiceInterface).

Try to poke a hole in the scope argument or the three-way split.

I tried; the core claim holds. The scope/lifecycle mismatch is the right axis, and your retry-loop caveat is correct, so I won't argue latency. A per-node imperative plugin genuinely cannot own a cluster-scoped, long-running, declarative provisioning lifecycle without inverting its own contract, so the three-way split by concern is the sound call.

The only reinforcement I'd add (not a hole): make the sub-operator's ownership of the DPF install non-destructive, if an operator already installed DPF, the sub-operator must adopt rather than recreate, and guard deletion with finalizers/resource-policy so tearing down a DpuOperatorConfig doesn't nuke a user-managed DPF. DPF already annotates some CRDs with helm.sh/resource-policy=keep, which supports this.

Agreed on the adopt-not-recreate guard, fold that into the risks section. Now formalize the trade-off exactly how a domain reviewer will want to see it: for each pattern, pure adapter, sub-operator, pure CRD-translation, and my hybrid, state specifically what it CAN carry and what it CANNOT carry, so it's self-evident why I assigned one concern to each rather than picking a single global pattern.

Then tabulate the lossy mapping edges. I already know the main ones: ServiceFunctionChain's flat {name,image} list can't express DPF's ServiceChainSet port/interface topology (forward-lossy, and non-linear DPF chains don't round-trip back, reverse-lossy), and SFC.NodeSelector selects host nodes while DPF selectors target DPU nodes (semantic shift). Add SetNumVfs (imperative) -> DPUFlavor (declarative) as a model/timing shift. Put it in tables.

Done, to your structure.

Trade-off by carrying capacity:
- Pure Adapter: CAN carry node-local device/VF/NF wiring with zero core change; CANNOT carry DPF's cluster-scoped async provisioning through a per-node plugin -> bottlenecks offload. Use for the node-local surface only.
- Sub-operator: CAN carry the DPF install + provisioning lifecycle declaratively; CANNOT cleanly serve the fast per-node imperative calls and would duplicate existing VF plumbing if used for everything. Use for the DPF lifecycle only.
- Pure CRD-translation: CAN carry declarative intent->DPF mapping; CANNOT integrate device/VF, so NVIDIA wouldn't be a real vendor -> parallel inconsistent path. Use for intent->DPF mapping only.
- Hybrid (your design): CAN carry every concern via its best-fit pattern with DPF reused wholesale and core essentially unchanged; the cost is more moving parts and a boundary that must be stated crisply (which the diagrams do).

Lossy edges tabulated: DpuOperatorConfig(nvidia) -> DPFOperatorConfig+DPUSet (1:N); ServiceFunctionChain -> DPUServiceChain (forward- and reverse-lossy on topology); SFC.NodeSelector -> DPUClusterSelector/ServiceChainSet NodeSelector (host-vs-DPU semantic shift); SetNumVfs -> DPUFlavor (imperative->declarative, effective next reconcile); CreateNetworkFunction/CreateBridgePort -> DPUServiceInterface/DPUServiceNAD (mostly 1:1).

Good. Now generate the Go skeleton to my spec, this is the bonus deliverable and it must compile.

Hard requirements:
- `go build` must pass OFFLINE. Do NOT import controller-runtime, DPF, or dpu-operator, mirror them with minimal local stub types and cite the real upstream import paths in comments. "Compilable but not necessarily functional" is the bar.
- Exactly the three components we agreed: DPFProvisioner (sub-operator, EnsureDPF + Ready), NVIDIAVSP (adapter), ServiceChainTranslator (a controller-runtime-shaped Reconcile(ctx, Request) (Result, error)).
- Add a compile-time assertion that *NVIDIAVSP satisfies the VendorPlugin interface (var _ VendorPlugin = (*NVIDIAVSP)(nil)).
- NVIDIAVSP.Start() must return a sentinel ErrDPFNotReady until DPFProvisioner.Ready() is true, so it mirrors the operator's real Init retry loop.
- Readiness must reference the real DPFOperatorConfig Ready condition (status.conditions[type=Ready]), NOT an invented field.
- TranslateSFC() must carry the lossy-mapping note as a code comment so implementers can't miss it.

Delivered as feature_skeleton.go (package nvidiavsp), to spec:
- Local controller-runtime-shaped stubs (NamespacedName/Request/Result/Object/Client/Reconciler) so it builds stdlib-only; each documented as a stand-in for the real sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime type.
- VendorPlugin interface mirroring internal/daemon/plugin.VendorPlugin, with `var _ VendorPlugin = (*NVIDIAVSP)(nil)`.
- DPFProvisioner.EnsureDPF() applies DPFOperatorConfig + DPUSet{BFB,DPUFlavor}; Ready() reads the DPFOperatorConfig Ready condition (comment notes real code also checks DPU/DPUSet provisioning status). The stub field is named Ready with a comment: "stands for status.conditions[type=Ready]".
- NVIDIAVSP.Start() returns ErrDPFNotReady until Provisioner.Ready(), matching the Init retry loop; Close/GetDevices/SetNumVfs/Create-DeleteNetworkFunction/SetDpuNetworkConfig implemented.
- ServiceChainTranslator.Reconcile() Gets the SFC and applies TranslateSFC(); TranslateSFC() carries the LOSSY MAPPING comment (forward/reverse-lossy topology + NodeSelector semantic shift).
Verify: `go build ./...` and `go vet ./...` both pass offline. Upstream type paths are cited at the top so each stub traces to source.

Now the diagrams for architecture_design.md. Requirement from the assignment is SEQUENCE diagrams (Mermaid sequenceDiagram), not flowcharts, respect that literally. I want the async Day-0 provisioning shown as a DISTINCT interaction from the runtime datapath wiring, because that separation is the entire justification for my hybrid boundary. Add a component/boundary view as a third diagram for context. In the provisioning diagram, annotate that readiness is tracked via the DPFOperatorConfig Ready condition + DPU/DPUSet status, not a blocking Init().

Provided three diagrams in architecture_design.md:
1. Component/boundary flowchart, OPI core (unchanged) | NVIDIA integration (sub-operator + VSP adapter + translator) | DPF (reused) -> BF3; shows which component applies which CRDs and that the VSP only reads DPFOperatorConfig readiness.
2. sequenceDiagram "Day-0 provisioning (async)", Admin -> OPI core -> DPF Provisioner -> DPF controllers -> BF3: ensure DPFOperatorConfig, apply DPUSet{BFB,DPUFlavor}, flash BFB, form DPUCluster; Note states minutes-long/declarative/async, tracked via DPFOperatorConfig Ready condition + DPU/DPUSet status, NOT a blocking Init().
3. sequenceDiagram "Runtime datapath wiring", OPI node daemon -> NVIDIA VSP: Init() returns ErrDPFNotReady then retries to ready; GetDevices/SetNumVfs; then a ServiceFunctionChain event -> ServiceChain Translator -> TranslateSFC -> apply DPUServiceChain + DPUServiceInterface -> DPF programs offloaded OVS-DOCA flows on BF3.
The async provisioning is kept visibly separate from the fast runtime path, which is exactly the boundary your hybrid is built on.

Two more things I want us to nail so this is reviewer-proof. First, pin the exact registration seam, I traced it while reading the daemon: internal/platform/vendordetector.go, NewDpuDetectorManager holds detectors []VendorDetector = { NewIntelDetector(), NewMarvellDetector(), // add more detectors here }, and each detector implements IsDpuPlatform, IsDPU, and VspPlugin(dpuMode, imageManager, client, pm, dpuIdentifier) (*plugin.GrpcPlugin, error). So NVIDIA is literally one new NvidiaBlueField3Detector at that line whose VspPlugin returns our adapter. Call that out as the single core-side edit, everything else stays out-of-tree.

Second, I want a Resilience and edge-cases section with naive/failure/fix discipline, but only failure modes specific to this hybrid, not generic operator hygiene. Here are the seven I care about and the fix I want for each, so you just formalize my reasoning: (1) VSP Init() racing ahead of DPF provisioning, gate Init on DPF readiness and return ErrDPFNotReady, lean on the existing retry loop. (2) sub-operator deleting a user's pre-existing DPF, adopt-not-own with a managed-by label plus resource-policy=keep, and draw the safe-deletion sequence. (3) both VSP and Translator writing svc.dpu.nvidia.com, single-writer per API group, Translator only, RBAC-enforced. (4) bidirectional CRD version skew since OPI is an evolving LF standard AND DPF ships on its own cadence, discovery on both CRD sets, a profile keyed on the (OPI, DPF) pair, UnsupportedVersion condition plus Event instead of a silent field-drop. (5) SetNumVfs imperative vs DPUFlavor declarative timing, return pending until DPU/DPUSet status reflects the count. (6) Translator applying a chain into the wrong cluster in DPF's two-cluster topology, a DPUCluster-selection convention from the SFC NodeSelector, refuse with TargetClusterUnresolved if ambiguous. (7) HA concurrent reconcilers, leader election plus idempotent full-desired-set SSA so a mid-fan-out eviction self-heals. Keep each one tight and add a sequence diagram for the deletion case.

Both done to your spec. Grounding: §2.1 now names the registry, NewDpuDetectorManager's detectors list with the literal // add more detectors here, the VendorDetector methods (IsDpuPlatform / IsDPU / VspPlugin(...) (*plugin.GrpcPlugin, error)), and states NVIDIA enters as one NvidiaBlueField3Detector whose VspPlugin returns the adapter, the single core-side edit with everything else out-of-tree.

Resilience §8 formalizes your seven, each as naive/failure/fix and each tied to the hybrid boundary rather than generic hygiene: 8.1 Init races DPF (ErrDPFNotReady gate on the existing retry); 8.2 adopt-not-own (managed-by label plus helm.sh/resource-policy=keep, which DPF already uses) with the safe-deletion sequence diagram you asked for; 8.3 single-writer on svc.dpu.nvidia.com (Translator only, SSA field-manager, RBAC-enforced); 8.4 bidirectional (OPI x DPF) version skew (discovery on both CRD sets, profile keyed on the version pair, UnsupportedVersion condition plus Event, never a silent field-drop); 8.5 SetNumVfs to DPUFlavor timing (pending until DPU/DPUSet status reflects the VF count); 8.6 wrong DPUCluster (selection convention from the SFC NodeSelector, refuse with TargetClusterUnresolved when ambiguous); 8.7 HA (leader-election Lease plus idempotent full-desired-set SSA so a mid-fan-out eviction self-heals). I folded the old risks list into these and kept §9 to the two genuinely-open questions, SFC topology expressiveness and Zero-Trust vs Host-Trusted mode.
